title: 1 Cash-Heavy Stock to Target This Week and 2 Facing Challenges
content:
Companies with more cash than debt can be financially resilient, but that doesn’t mean they’re all strong investments. Some lack leverage because they struggle to grow or generate consistent profits, making them unattractive borrowers.

Financial flexibility is valuable, but it’s not everything - at StockStory, we help you find the stocks that can not only survive but also outperform. Keeping that in mind, here is one company with a net cash position that balances growth with stability and two best left off your watchlist.

Two Stocks to Sell:

UFP Industries (UFPI)

Net Cash Position: $684.4 million (12.6% of Market Cap)

Beginning as a lumber supplier in the 1950s, UFP Industries (NASDAQ:UFPI) is a holding company making building materials for the construction, retail, and industrial sectors.

Why Are We Out on UFPI?

Products and services are facing significant end-market challenges during this cycle as sales have declined by 6.4% annually over the last two years Earnings per share decreased by more than its revenue over the last two years, showing each sale was less profitable Waning returns on capital imply its previous profit engines are losing steam

UFP Industries’s stock price of $95.50 implies a valuation ratio of 17.8x forward P/E. If you’re considering UFPI for your portfolio, see our FREE research report to learn more.

CME Group (CME)

Net Cash Position: $938.8 million (0.9% of Market Cap)

Born from the Chicago Mercantile Exchange founded in 1898 as a butter and egg trading venue, CME Group (NASDAQ:CME) operates the world's largest derivatives marketplace where traders can buy and sell futures and options contracts across interest rates, equities, currencies, commodities, and more.

Why Are We Hesitant About CME?

Annual revenue growth of 6% over the last five years was below our standards for the financials sector Earnings growth over the last two years fell short of the peer group average as its EPS only increased by 9.6% annually

CME Group is trading at $297.07 per share, or 24.3x forward P/E. To fully understand why you should be careful with CME, check out our full research report (it’s free).

One Stock to Watch:

IonQ (IONQ)

Net Cash Position: $2.36 billion (22.4% of Market Cap)

Founded by quantum physics pioneers from the University of Maryland and Duke University in 2015, IonQ (NYSE:IONQ) develops quantum computers that process information using trapped ions to solve complex computational problems beyond the capabilities of traditional computers.

Why Do We Like IONQ?

Market share has increased this cycle as its 143% annual revenue growth over the last two years was exceptional Expected revenue growth of 81.7% for the next year suggests its market share will rise Adjusted operating margin improvement of 1,226.6 percentage points over the last five years demonstrates its ability to scale efficiently

title: Is It Time To Reassess UFP Industries (UFPI) After Recent Share Price Strength
content:
Find winning stocks in any market cycle. Join 7 million investors using Simply Wall St's investing ideas for FREE.

If you are wondering whether UFP Industries at around US$95.49 offers good value today, the key is to separate price moves from what the business is actually worth. The stock has returned 5.7% over the last 7 days and 5.4% over the past month, while the 1 year return sits at a 9.0% decline and longer term 3 year and 5 year returns are 26.1% and 24.8% respectively. Recent attention on UFP Industries has been driven by ongoing interest in the building products space and how companies are positioned for future demand and capital allocation decisions. This context helps explain why the share price has been more active recently, as investors reassess both risk and potential reward. UFP Industries currently has a valuation score of 5 out of 6. Next up is a look at how different valuation methods line up on the stock and why there may be an even better way to think about value by the end of this article.

Find out why UFP Industries's -9.0% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Approach 1: UFP Industries Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s value, aiming to estimate what the entire business might be worth right now.

For UFP Industries, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow sits at about $332.3 million, and analyst inputs combined with extrapolations extend out to a projected free cash flow of roughly $764.3 million in 2035. Estimates within this path include $181.9 million in 2026, $219.9 million in 2027, and $343 million in 2028, with later years extrapolated by Simply Wall St beyond the analyst horizon.

When all of these projected cash flows are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $172.45 per share for UFP Industries. Compared with the recent share price of around $95.49, the DCF output suggests the stock is trading at a 44.6% discount and appears materially undervalued on this cash flow view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: UFP Industries Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it tells you how much you are paying for each dollar of current earnings. Higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk usually support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk typically lines up with a lower, more conservative multiple.

Story Continues

UFP Industries currently trades on a P/E of about 19.12x. That sits below the Building industry average of roughly 21.05x and well below the peer group average of about 34.99x, which on the surface can make the stock look relatively inexpensive compared with other companies.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for UFP Industries is 22.35x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a more tailored P/E might look like after factoring in the company’s earnings growth profile, its industry, profit margins, market capitalization and key risks. Because it is built around company specific characteristics rather than broad group averages, the Fair Ratio can give a clearer read than a simple comparison with peers or the wider sector. Set against the current P/E of 19.12x, the Fair Ratio suggests UFP Industries may be trading below what could be considered a more balanced earnings multiple.

title: Building Materials Stocks Q4 Earnings: Carlisle (NYSE:CSL) Firing on All Cylinders
content:
The end of the earnings season is always a good time to take a step back and see who shined (and who not so much). Let’s take a look at how building materials stocks fared in Q4, starting with Carlisle (NYSE:CSL).

Traditionally, building materials companies have built competitive advantages with economies of scale, brand recognition, and strong relationships with builders and contractors. More recently, advances to address labor availability and job site productivity have spurred innovation. Additionally, companies in the space that can produce more energy-efficient materials have opportunities to take share. However, these companies are at the whim of construction volumes, which tend to be cyclical and can be impacted heavily by economic factors such as interest rates. Additionally, the costs of raw materials can be driven by a myriad of worldwide factors and greatly influence the profitability of building materials companies.

The 9 building materials stocks we track reported a slower Q4. As a group, revenues missed analysts’ consensus estimates by 1.2% while next quarter’s revenue guidance was in line.

Amidst this news, share prices of the companies have had a rough stretch. On average, they are down 11.4% since the latest earnings results.

Best Q4: Carlisle (NYSE:CSL)

Originally founded as Carlisle Tire and Rubber Company, Carlisle Companies (NYSE:CSL) is a multi-industry product manufacturer focusing on construction materials and weatherproofing technologies.

Carlisle reported revenues of $1.13 billion, flat year on year. This print exceeded analysts’ expectations by 1.4%. Overall, it was a very strong quarter for the company with a solid beat of analysts’ adjusted operating income estimates.Carlisle Total Revenue

The stock is down 6.9% since reporting and currently trades at $331.24.

Is now the time to buy Carlisle? Access our full analysis of the earnings results here, it’s free.

Sherwin-Williams (NYSE:SHW)

Widely known for its success in the paint industry, Sherwin-Williams (NYSE:SHW) is a manufacturer of paints, coatings, and related products.

Sherwin-Williams reported revenues of $5.60 billion, up 5.6% year on year, outperforming analysts’ expectations by 0.8%. The business had a satisfactory quarter with an impressive beat of analysts’ EBITDA estimates but full-year EPS guidance missing analysts’ expectations significantly.Sherwin-Williams Total Revenue

Although it had a fine quarter compared to its peers, the market seems unhappy with the results as the stock is down 9.5% since reporting. It currently trades at $316.53.

Is now the time to buy Sherwin-Williams? Access our full analysis of the earnings results here, it’s free.

Story Continues

Slowest Q4: UFP Industries (NASDAQ:UFPI)

Beginning as a lumber supplier in the 1950s, UFP Industries (NASDAQ:UFPI) is a holding company making building materials for the construction, retail, and industrial sectors.

UFP Industries reported revenues of $1.33 billion, down 9% year on year, falling short of analysts’ expectations by 5%. It was a disappointing quarter as it posted a significant miss of analysts’ revenue and adjusted operating income estimates.

UFP Industries delivered the slowest revenue growth in the group. As expected, the stock is down 14.5% since the results and currently trades at $90.96.

Read our full analysis of UFP Industries’s results here.

Tecnoglass (NYSE:TGLS)

The first-ever Colombian company to trade on the NASDAQ, Tecnoglass (NYSE:TGLS) is a manufacturer of architectural glass, windows, and aluminum products.

Tecnoglass reported revenues of $245.3 million, up 2.4% year on year. This number beat analysts’ expectations by 1.7%. More broadly, it was a softer quarter as it logged full-year EBITDA guidance missing analysts’ expectations.

Tecnoglass delivered the biggest analyst estimates beat among its peers. The stock is down 11% since reporting and currently trades at $43.70.

Read our full, actionable report on Tecnoglass here, it’s free.

Valmont (NYSE:VMI)

Credited with an invention in the 1950s that improved crop yields, Valmont (NYSE:VMI) provides engineered products and infrastructure services for the agricultural industry.

Valmont reported revenues of $1.04 billion, flat year on year. This print lagged analysts' expectations by 0.7%. More broadly, it was a mixed quarter as it also recorded a beat of analysts’ EPS estimates but a significant miss of analysts’ adjusted operating income estimates.

The stock is down 15.5% since reporting and currently trades at $401.87.

Read our full, actionable report on Valmont here, it’s free.

Market Update

Late in 2025 into early 2026, there was hand wringing around artificial intelligence. For software companies, the fear was that AI would erode pricing power and compress margins as new tools made it easier to replicate what once required expensive enterprise platforms. Crypto investors had their own version of the same anxiety: if AI agents could trade, allocate capital, and manage wallets autonomously, what exactly was the long-term value of today’s crypto infrastructure?

These concerns triggered a noticeable rotation away from these sectors and into safer havens. But markets rarely dwell on one narrative for long. Spring 2026 came, and the focus shifted abruptly from technological disruption to geopolitical risk. The US’ conflict with Iran became the dominant driver of market psychology, and when geopolitics takes center stage, the script changes quickly. Investors stop debating growth rates and start worrying about oil supply, inflation, and global stability.

title: Can Deckorators Deal Boost UFP Industries' Position in Outdoor Living?
content:
UFP Industries, Inc. UFPI is strengthening its position in outdoor living through a strategic move by its Deckorators division. The company acquired the remaining operating assets of MoistureShield, a well-known brand in the decking market.

The acquisition marks a step to expand wood plastic composite manufacturing capabilities, enhance the product portfolio and support faster innovation. The Arkansas facility adds strong expertise in traditional composite decking. This complements Deckorators’ Surestone technology and design capabilities, improving the overall offering.

The combined platform is expected to deliver broader product options and better value to dealers, contractors, distributors and homeowners. The company also gains proprietary CoolDeck technology, which helps reduce heat absorption compared with standard composite decking. This addition strengthens product differentiation in a competitive market.

The deal also supports capacity expansion. The facility provides immediate room for additional sales and future scaling opportunities. The company expects that with further investments, Deckorators’ WPC capacity could double to $200 million by 2027. This reflects a clear growth path supported by manufacturing expansion.

Demand in outdoor living remains strong as homeowners continue to invest in outdoor spaces for daily use and leisure. The company plans to use this demand to expand product offerings, strengthen dealer and contractor reach, and accelerate product development.

In the fourth quarter of 2025, the company highlighted steady demand trends across key segments. Overall, the Deckorators deal appears to position UFP Industries to capture rising demand, expand the product mix and strengthen its competitive standing in outdoor living markets.

How UFP Industries Stacks Up Against Industry Peers

UFP Industries operates in a competitive outdoor living and composite decking market. The company faces competition from players such as Trex Company, Inc. TREX and Simpson Manufacturing Co., Inc. SSD, both of which serve key parts of the decking and construction value chain.

Trex remains a major competitor with a strong presence in composite decking and railing products. The company continues to benefit from steady demand for low-maintenance and sustainable outdoor solutions. Trex focuses on product innovation and brand strength, which supports its leadership in the composite decking space.

Simpson Manufacturing supplies structural connectors, fasteners and building solutions used in decking and outdoor construction. The company benefits from steady residential construction and repair activity. Simpson Manufacturing’s products play a critical role in supporting deck installations and outdoor structures.

UFP Industries is strengthening its position through the Deckorators acquisition. The added capacity, expanded product mix and new technologies may help the company compete more effectively across the outdoor living value chain.

title: Deckorators® Signs Agreement to Acquire Expanded Manufacturing Capabilities, Accelerating Innovation in Outdoor Living
content:
GRAND RAPIDS, Mich., April 6, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Deckorators®, a leading brand in the outdoor living industry and a division of UFP Industries, today announced it has acquired the remaining operating assets for MoistureShield decking, a respected brand in the decking and outdoor living market.Deckorators, the first name in decking, railing and accessories, invented the low-maintenance aluminum balusters category and has since led the industry with innovative decking and railing products. With dependably on-trend designs, Deckorators lets DIYers and builders extend their creative ideas from a home’s interior to its outdoor living spaces. Deckorators is a brand of UFP Retail Solutions, LLC, a UFP Industries company.

The acquisition represents a strategic step forward for Deckorators as it continues to expand its wood plastic composite manufacturing capabilities, strengthen its product portfolio, and accelerate innovation in the rapidly growing outdoor living category.

The newly acquired Arkansas facility brings strong expertise in traditional composite decking, complementing Deckorators' industry-leading Surestone® technology and design leadership. Together, the combined organization will be well positioned to deliver expanded product offerings and enhanced value to dealers, contractors, distributors, and homeowners.

"The addition of MoistureShield strengthens our ability to innovate and serve our customers," said Ryan Kemp, Executive Vice President of Deckorators. "By combining our technologies, product development capabilities, and market reach, we will accelerate growth and continue delivering high-performance outdoor solutions."

The outdoor living category continues to see strong demand as homeowners invest in outdoor spaces for relaxation, entertainment, and everyday living. Deckorators plans to leverage the acquisition to expand its product offerings, strengthen its reach with dealers and contractors, and accelerate product development across its decking portfolio.

"This acquisition allows us to support customers more effectively today while creating the runway we need to expand our product mix and grow the Deckorators brand over the long term," said Landon Tarvin, President of UFP Retail Solutions, which includes the Deckorators brand in its portfolio. "The facility provides immediate capacity for additional sales with opportunities to add capacity in the future as sales growth objectives are achieved. We expect that with both this investment and additional capital investments at this location, we will have the ability to double Deckorators' overall capacity of WPC to a total of $200M by 2027."

The purchase strengthens Deckorators' core business with the acquisition of manufacturing assets, certain product brands and proprietary CoolDeck® composite decking technology— which is designed to reduce heat absorption compared to standard composite decking— previously operated by Oldcastle APG.

Story Continues

About Deckorators

Deckorators, the first name in decking, railing and accessories, invented the low-maintenance aluminum balusters category and has since led the industry with innovative decking and railing products. With dependably on-trend designs, Deckorators lets DIYers and builders extend their creative ideas from a home's interior to its outdoor living spaces. Deckorators is a brand of UFP Retail Solutions, LLC, a UFP Industries company.

To learn more about Deckorators® decking and railing accessories, visit www.deckorators.com or call 800-556-8449.

Follow Deckorators on:

Instagram: @deckorators  LinkedIn: linkedin.com/company/deckorators  Facebook: facebook.com/deckorators  YouTube: youtube.com/deckoratorsproducts  Pinterest: pinterest.com/deckorators

UFP Industries, Inc.(NASDAQ: UFPI)

UFP Industries, Inc., formerly Universal Forest Products, is a holding company whose operating subsidiaries—UFP Packaging, UFP Construction and UFP Retail Solutions—manufacture, distribute, and sell a wide variety of wood and alternative material building and industrial products worldwide. Founded in 1 955, the company is headquartered in Grand Rapids, Michigan, with affiliates throughout North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia. title: UFP Industries Expands Deckorators® Composite Decking Capacity Through Acquisition
content:
GRAND RAPIDS, Mich., April 6, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- UFP Industries today announced the acquisition of Oldcastle APG's MoistureShield decking operating assets, a strategic transaction that significantly expands Deckorators® wood-plastic composite (WPC) decking capacity.UFP Industries, Inc. (PRNewsfoto/UFP Industries, Inc.)

This acquisition strengthens a core business for UFP Industries and positions Deckorators for long-term growth in the outdoor living market. The purchase further enhances Deckorators' product portfolio with the acquisition of manufacturing assets, inventory, certain product brands and proprietary CoolDeck® composite decking technology— which is designed to reduce heat absorption compared to standard composite decking.

More broadly, the added capacity enables Deckorators to scale production—positioning the brand to better serve customers and support new and innovative product introductions in this growing and attractive market.

"This acquisition is fundamentally about our commitment to strengthen our core business and adding capacity to execute our growth plans," said Will Schwartz, CEO of UFP Industries. "By adding proven manufacturing assets and complementary technologies to the Deckorators business, we are removing constraints, expanding capacity and putting the brand in a stronger position to meet demand. One of our strategic objectives is to expand Deckorators' production capacity geographically. This was an attractive investment opportunity that met our internal return targets and will allow us to meet these growth plans ahead of greenfield expansion opportunities."

The acquired operations will be integrated into Deckorators' existing manufacturing network, enhancing operational resilience and creating opportunities to optimize production across locations, while selectively incorporating additional composite technologies into the Deckorators lineup. Oldcastle APG will retain and continue operating their RDI® Railing and Catalyst™ Fence Solutions brands.

As demand for composite decking continues to rise, the added capacity allows Deckorators to better support national retail partners, dealers and contractors while maintaining focus on innovation, quality, and service.

"This acquisition allows us to support customers more effectively today while creating the runway we need to expand our product mix and grow the Deckorators brand over the long term," said Landon Tarvin, President of UFP Retail Solutions, which includes the Deckorators brand in its portfolio. "The facility provides immediate capacity for additional sales with opportunities to add capacity in the future as sales growth objectives are achieved. We expect that with both this investment and additional capital investments at this location, we will have the ability to double Deckorators' overall capacity of WPC to a total of $200M by 2027."

Story Continues

The acquisition underscores UFP Industries' continued focus on investing in core businesses, expanding capacity in high-growth categories, and strengthening the competitive position of its leading brands.Cision

title: UFP Industries: A Through-Cycle Compounder at a Fair Price
content:
This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

Introduction

UFP Industries is best understood as a long-term compounding business whose end markets happen to include housing, not as a commodity lumber proxy that periodically earns windfall profits. Over multiple cycles, the company has remained profitable, grown book value per share, and expanded earnings through a combination of value-added manufacturing, disciplined acquisitions, and steady share count reduction. That is the investment lens that matters here. At roughly 21 trailing earnings and about 2 book value, the market is not pricing distress. It is asking whether UFP can continue compounding per-share results at a double-digit rate through the cycle.

Is FRA:UFP fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

The long-term record suggests that question is worth asking. UFP remained profitable even through the housing collapse and financial crisis, and over the last decade earnings per share and book value per share have expanded materially. That progress has not come from a single commodity tailwind. It has come from a broader operating base spanning retail, packaging, and construction markets, along with a product mix that has shifted further toward value-added categories where manufacturing capability, logistics, and customer relationships matter more than raw material spreads alone.

That framing also clarifies the valuation debate. If UFP can continue earning low-to-mid-teens returns on equity through the cycle and reinvest capital without weakening the balance sheet, a valuation in this range can still support satisfactory long-term returns. If margins revert fully to the pre-2020 baseline and capital discipline weakens, the compounding case thins out quickly. The investment case, then, is not about a near-term rerating. It is about whether the company's through-cycle return profile is durable enough to justify paying a fair price for a steady compounding business.

Business Model

UFP Industries is a materials manufacturing and distribution company focused on delivering value-added products across multiple end markets rather than acting as a pure commodity lumber supplier. Founded in 1955 and based in Grand Rapids, Michigan, the company operates through three core segments, Retail Solutions, Packaging, and Construction, each of which serves large addressable market niches with differentiated offerings and customer proximity. Retail Solutions is the largest contributor to company revenue and centers on products sold through national home centers, independent lumberyards, and contractor channels. This segment manufactures and distributes pressure-treated lumber, fencing, decking and other exterior products under ProWood, UFP-Edge and Deckorators brands, with UFP consistently ranking among the largest producers of several of these products in the U.S.

Story Continues

The Packaging segment, formerly referred to as the Industrial segment, encompasses engineered and structural packaging solutions, custom pallet manufacturing through PalletOne, and protective packaging designed to meet specific customer requirements. These products are typically sold to industrial customers that require custom material handling and logistics components, a market with different demand drivers than residential construction alone. The Construction segment spans Factory-Built Housing, Site-Built Construction, Commercial Construction and concrete forming products. In Factory-Built Housing, UFP produces engineered roof trusses, dimensional lumber and related componentry for modular and manufactured housing, with a notable share of production in the U.S. concrete forming and site construction markets.

Importantly, this tri-segment structure provides diversification across end markets, which UFP management describes as an inherent hedge against market volatility, a point the company emphasizes in its annual disclosures as a means of stabilizing earnings and cash flow.

The revenue mix further reinforces this diversified footprint. Industry data estimates segment contributions in 2024 as roughly 41% Retail, 33% Site-Built Construction, and 26% Industrial/Packaging, with the company's U.S. operations accounting for the overwhelming share of sales. This geographic concentration underscores the company's deep integration with North American residential and industrial markets.

UFP's product strategy is intentionally tilted toward value-added offerings. Beyond raw lumber, the company has expanded its portfolio into composite materials and engineered goods, such as mineral-based decking under the Deckorators brand, and pursues new product introductions that are increasingly targeted to higher-margin niches. The company's long-term goal of at least 10% of sales coming from new products is documented in recent filings, and new offerings have represented a meaningful portion of early-2025 results. This value-added focus distinguishes UFP from traditional lumber companies whose earnings may be heavily driven by commodity cycles. Because many of UFP's products are engineered or branded, the company can leverage design, manufacturing scale, and distribution logistics to support more stable pricing and lower reliance on raw commodity swings. These factors matter for owners because they influence margin persistence through economic cycles.

UFP's operating model also emphasizes a wide physical footprint and supply chain infrastructure. With more than 200 facilities across North America and globally, the company maintains proximity to customers, which reduces lead times and logistics cost inefficiencies and supports integrated service delivery across its segments. While demand in any individual segment can recede, for example, retail building materials during a housing slowdown, the diversified mix and value-added orientation provide a degree of natural hedging. Unlike single-market operators, UFP's earnings reflect contributions from both industrial customers and broad construction activities, and management's strategic emphasis on innovation and new product growth supports revenue resilience.

Ultimately, the economic engine of UFP Industries is not merely the cyclical demand for raw materials. It is the company's ability to deliver engineered solutions across multiple markets, capture margin through differentiated products, and leverage distribution scale to maintain earnings through periods of softness. In the context of long-term compounding, this diversified, value-added model is a structural foundation for the company's ability to grow earnings and book value per share across cycles.UFP Industries: A Through-Cycle Compounder at a Fair Price

Structural vs. Cyclical Profitability: What Changed, and What Can Revert

The easiest way to misread UFP Industries is to look at its pre-2020 margin history and assume the last few years were simply a commodity windfall. For much of the 20162019 period, operating margins sat in the mid-single digits, and the business looked like a well-run but inherently cyclical participant in wood-based end markets. That backdrop is exactly why the post-2020 profitability reset creates skepticism: the margin step-up is too large to accept on faith.

The first point to separate is price level from economic capture. Lumber inflation can inflate reported revenue without improving underlying economics; value-added manufacturing and better operating discipline can expand gross profit dollars even when revenue normalizes. What stands out in the recent history is that operating margins did not simply spike during the most extreme pricing conditions and then collapse back to the old baseline. Operating margins rose meaningfully in 2020 and 2021, peaked around 2022, eased in 2023, and then moved lower again in 2024, but still remained above the pre-2020 range. In other words, the company's downcycle profitability in the last two years has been better than what used to be considered normal. Management's own language in the annual report supports the interpretation that the company now runs a tighter operating model through volume and pricing declines than it did historically. One specific disclosure is telling: in the 2024 annual report, the company calculates a decremental operating margin of 27.3%, meaning operating profit fell about $0.27 for every $1.00 decline in sales from 2023 to 2024. That is not a perfect metric, but it gives owners a concrete way to think about how much profitability is retained when pricing and demand soften. A business that is purely a commodity spread player tends to see profitability collapse faster than revenue in a down leg; UFP is explicitly showing that it retains a meaningful portion of earnings even as sales decline.

There are also clues in cost structure that support a higher baseline than the old mid-single digits forever view. The annual report discusses SG&A relative to gross profit and compares today's cost structure to 2019 as a reference point before the pandemic. That matters because if the company can hold operating expenses in check while maintaining value-added gross profit dollars, the margin floor naturally rises. In 2024, the company also discloses inbound and outbound transportation costs as a percentage of sales, highlighting how operating inputs and logistics intensity shift across the cycle. These details do not prove permanence, but they reinforce that the company's post-2020 discipline is not only the residue of elevated pricing. A fair counterpoint is that 2024 demonstrates the other side of the argument: profitability is not immune. As pricing pressure intensified and sales declined, margins compressed. Recent quarterly reporting in 2025 continues that theme: sales and margins have been down year-over-year, with gross margin and adjusted EBITDA margin stepping lower versus the prior year. That is the sober way to frame the debate: UFP's economics appear improved versus the pre-2020 baseline, but the durability of that improvement depends on two variables owners should keep separate, mix stability (how much value-added business remains structurally larger) and pricing discipline (how much margin can be defended when demand softens).

The reason this matters for valuation is simple. If the market assumes a full reversion back to the old operating margin range, the stock will trade like a housing/commodity cyclical no matter how much the business has diversified. If the more realistic underwriting is that margins have reset to a higher through-cycle band, lower than the 20212022 peak, but higher than 20162019, then today's multiple should be anchored to that normalized earnings base rather than to peak fear or peak exuberance.

What sits underneath that margin discussion is the way capital has been deployed over time. UFP has not treated periods of elevated profitability as an opportunity to scale indiscriminately. Instead, capital allocation has been directed toward reinforcing the parts of the business that support higher through-cycle margins. Acquisitions have generally been small and capability-driven, extending product offerings or deepening customer relationships rather than adding raw capacity. At the same time, reinvestment has been concentrated in value-added operations, where design, manufacturing, and logistics capabilities allow the company to capture more gross profit per unit of volume. Share repurchases have gradually reduced the share count over time, allowing operating improvements to translate more directly into earnings per share. Importantly, this has been done without stretching the balance sheet, with management maintaining a conservative leverage profile even through periods of strong demand. The result is that the improvement in margins is not only an operating story, but also a capital allocation story: a larger portion of earnings is now being generated from higher-quality activities and then retained on a per-share basis.UFP Industries: A Through-Cycle Compounder at a Fair Price

Valuation: Underwriting Durability, Not Cyclicality

At recent trading levels near $115$120 per share and approximately 3839 million diluted shares outstanding, UFP Industries carries an equity market capitalization in the range of $4.4$4.6 billion. Fiscal 2024 diluted earnings per share were approximately $5.60, placing the stock at roughly 2021 trailing earnings. Book value per share at year-end 2024 was approximately $58, implying a price-to-book ratio close to 2.

Those figures are important because they frame the discussion correctly. A business trading at 20 earnings and 2 book is not being valued as a distressed or deeply cyclical enterprise. The market is already assigning credit for resilience and a degree of structural improvement relative to pre-2020 conditions. The question, therefore, is not whether UFP is misunderstood as cyclical. The question is what rate of per-share earnings growth justifies a 20 multiple.

The arithmetic is uncomplicated. A company that compounds earnings at 15% annually doubles earnings roughly every five years. At 12%, earnings double in about six years. At 10%, the doubling period stretches to just over seven years. If earnings were to double every five years and the valuation multiple remained constant at 20, the share price would also approximately double over that same period, implying a mid-teens annualized return before any change in valuation. In effect, today's price embeds an assumption that UFP can grow earnings at a low-to-mid-teens rate through the cycle without impairing returns on capital.

Whether that assumption is reasonable depends on the relationship between return on invested capital and reinvestment. Long-run earnings growth is a function of how much capital the company can deploy at attractive incremental returns. UFP has historically generated mid-teens returns on equity outside of extreme lumber price environments. Even in normalization periods, profitability has remained solidly positive, and the company maintained profitability through the housing collapse, the financial crisis, and the post-pandemic lumber unwind. That record matters more than any single year's margin volatility.

Reinvestment has been disciplined rather than speculative. The company has expanded through bolt-on acquisitions, internal capacity investments, and mix shift toward value-added segments such as retail solutions and industrial packaging, but it has not pursued balance sheet expansion that would jeopardize resilience in a downturn. Net debt remains modest relative to earnings capacity, and capital allocation has tended to emphasize flexibility rather than leverage-driven acceleration.

If UFP can sustain mid-teens returns on equity and reinvest a meaningful portion of earnings without dilution or balance sheet strain, then low-to-mid-teens EPS growth is not an aggressive assumption. Under that scenario, a 20 multiple is neither obviously cheap nor obviously excessive; it reflects a steady compounding business with cyclical exposure but structural discipline. If, however, returns on capital revert materially lower or reinvestment becomes less productive, the embedded growth assumption weakens, and the current multiple would imply more modest forward returns.

The valuation therefore does not hinge on whether housing activity improves next quarter or whether lumber prices normalize further. It hinges on durability. At approximately 20 earnings, investors are underwriting sustained capital discipline and mid-teens return characteristics over long periods. The investment debate is less about misclassification and more about whether that durability is earned.UFP Industries: A Through-Cycle Compounder at a Fair Price

The return on equity profile illustrates both cyclicality and durability. The 20212022 spike reflects extraordinary lumber spreads rather than structural economics. What matters more is the behavior outside that window. From 2014 through 2019, ROE generally ranged between roughly 10% and 14%, despite fluctuations in housing activity and commodity input costs. Even during the post-pandemic unwind, returns remained positive and well above distress levels. The recent normalization into high single digits does not resemble structural impairment; it resembles margin reversion toward historical through-cycle averages. If long-term returns cluster around low-to-mid teens in normal environments and the balance sheet remains disciplined, the compounding math underlying a 20 earnings multiple becomes less speculative and more mechanical.

Ownership

The shareholder base reinforces the idea that UFP Industries is being evaluated on normalized earnings power rather than short-term housing momentum. The largest disclosed position among the funds shown is Royce Associates, which owns roughly 806,000 shares, representing about 0.74% of its portfolio, valued at approximately $73 million. The fund increased its position modestly in the latest period (+4.95%). Royce has historically focused on smaller industrial companies with tangible asset backing and mid-cycle profitability characteristics, which aligns with how this business should be underwritten.

Dimensional Fund Advisors holds roughly 1.91 million shares, valued near $174 million, and added about 2.4% to the position in the most recent filing period. Dimensional's models typically emphasize profitability and valuation factors, so incremental buying here suggests the company screens attractively on those dimensions despite the cyclical overlay narrative.

There has also been meaningful position building from several multi-strategy managers. Schonfeld Strategic Advisorsincreased its stake sharply from a small base, while Millennium Management raised its position significantly, now holding more than 425,000 shares. Magnetar Financial increased its stake by roughly 61%, and Hotchkis & Wileyinitiated a new position. At the same time, Renaissance Technologies (Trades, Portfolio) trimmed its holdings by about 20%, which appears more consistent with tactical rebalancing than a thesis reversal.

What stands out is not aggressive concentration, but steady accumulation by valuation-oriented capital. The average buy prices displayed generally range from the high-$70s to around $100 per share, suggesting that many of these positions were established during periods when the market was skeptical about housing-linked exposure. That context matters. These managers were not underwriting peak enthusiasm; they were underwriting durability.

The shareholder list does not reflect momentum-driven crowding or thematic over-ownership. Instead, it shows participation from disciplined investors who tend to focus on balance sheet strength, normalized return on equity, and capital allocation track record. That composition fits the investment case: this is not a story stock, but a steady industrial compounder with a demonstrated habit of returning capital when incremental reinvestment opportunities do not justify expansion.

Risks

Owning UFP Industries is not a bet on lumber prices alone. It is a bet that the company's post-2020 margin structure, capital discipline, and value-added mix represent a higher baseline than the pre-pandemic era. The real risk is that this proves overstated.

Customer Concentration and Retail Dependence

UFP discloses that Home Depot and Lowe's together represented approximately 28% of fiscal 2024 net sales. That level of concentration is material. If either customer alters sourcing strategy, negotiates pricing more aggressively, reduces SKU count, or vertically integrates product lines, revenue and margin impact would be immediate.

This is not theoretical. Large retailers routinely pressure suppliers during slower housing periods. Even if unit demand remains stable, pricing concessions can compress margins quickly. The bull case assumes UFP's value-added positioning gives it pricing resilience. The risk is that scale buyers use their leverage during softer demand periods to reclaim economics.

Margin Normalization Risk

Operating margins peaked during 20212022 when lumber prices and supply chain constraints favored producers. While margins have remained above pre-2020 levels, they have declined meaningfully from peak levels as pricing normalized. Management disclosed a 27.3% decremental operating margin in 2024, meaning roughly $0.27 of operating income was lost for every $1.00 of revenue decline year-over-year. That demonstrates cost discipline, but it also confirms earnings remain sensitive to top-line pressure.

If end markets remain sluggish for longer, particularly in repair-and-remodel and new construction, margins could compress further toward historical mid-single-digit levels. The thesis breaks if normalized operating margin proves closer to 56% rather than 79%.

Commodity and Pass-Through Timing Gaps

While many of UFP's products include pass-through pricing mechanisms for lumber inputs, pass-through is rarely instantaneous. The company itself highlights raw material price volatility as a key business risk in its filings.In rapidly falling lumber markets, inventory purchased at higher costs can pressure gross margin. In rising markets, customer resistance can delay pricing recovery. This lag effect can distort earnings materially even if long-term spreads remain intact. Owners are underwriting pricing discipline. The risk lies in timing mismatch.

Packaging Segment Cyclicality

The Packaging segment is often viewed as diversification away from housing. However, pallet and protective packaging demand is tied closely to industrial production and freight activity. Recent earnings commentary has pointed to competitive pricing pressure within Packaging, particularly during slower industrial periods. If manufacturing activity remains weak or inventory cycles continue to normalize, that segment may not provide the stabilizing effect investors expect.

Capital Allocation Risk

One of UFP's core strengths has been restraint. The company ended fiscal 2024 with strong liquidity and modest net debt. It has repurchased shares and avoided large, transformative acquisitions. But capital discipline is cyclical by nature. The temptation to deploy cash into expansion or acquisitions during perceived recovery phases is always present. If management over-invests at the wrong point in the cycle, returns on invested capital would compress quickly.

Conclusion

The debate around UFP Industries is often framed as a question of cyclicality, but at the current valuation that framing is incomplete. At roughly 20 earnings and near 2 book value, the market is not pricing a recovery from distress. It is pricing a business expected to sustain steady, through-cycle earnings growth while maintaining acceptable returns on capital.

That shifts the analysis from narrative to underwriting. If UFP can continue generating low-to-mid-teens returns on equity and reinvest capital with discipline, then earnings can compound at a similar rate over time. At that level of growth, a ~20 multiple does not require expansion to produce reasonable long-term returns. The outcome is driven by the consistency of the compounding engine rather than by changes in sentiment.

The risk is equally clear. If returns on capital revert meaningfully toward lower historical ranges or incremental capital is deployed at weaker returns, the embedded growth in the current multiple becomes harder to justify. In that case, valuation compresses not because the business is mischaracterized, but because the underlying economics no longer support the expected rate of compounding.

The long-term record provides the relevant context. UFP has remained profitable through housing downturns and periods of commodity volatility, while continuing to grow book value and earnings per share over time. That history does not eliminate cyclicality, but it does suggest that the business has evolved into a more resilient operator than its end markets alone would imply.

At today's price, the investment case does not depend on a near-term inflection in housing or commodity conditions. It depends on whether UFP can continue converting its operating model and capital allocation discipline into steady per-share growth over the next decade. At this valuation, the thesis is straightforward: if the compounding holds, the returns follow.

title: Why Is UFP Industries (UFPI) Down 16.2% Since Last Earnings Report?
content:
It has been about a month since the last earnings report for UFP Industries (UFPI). Shares have lost about 16.2% in that time frame, underperforming the S&P 500.

Will the recent negative trend continue leading up to its next earnings release, or is UFP Industries due for a breakout? Well, first let's take a quick look at its most recent earnings report in order to get a better handle on the recent catalysts for UFP Industries, Inc. before we dive into how investors and analysts have reacted as of late.

UFP Industries Q4 Earnings & Sales Miss Estimates, Both Down Y/Y

UFP Industries reported lower than expected fourth-quarter 2025 results, with adjusted earnings and net sales missing the Zacks Consensus Estimate and also declining year over year.

Earnings & Sales Performance in Q4

Adjusted EPS of 70 cents missed the Zacks Consensus Estimate of $1.03 by 32%. In the year-ago quarter, it had reported an EPS of $1.12.

Quarterly net sales of $1.33 billion missed the consensus mark of $1.4 billion by 5.3% and declined by 9% year over year from $1.46 billion.

UFPI’s Margins & Profitability

Gross profit totaled $216.5 million, down from $239.5 million in the year-ago quarter, with gross margin contracting to 16.3% from 16.4% a year earlier.

Adjusted EBITDA came in at $107 million, down from $132.7 million. Adjusted EBITDA margin contracted to 8.1% from 9.1% year over year.

Q4 Segment Highlights

UFP Retail Solutions: Net sales of $444 million, down 15.4% from last year. Segment adjusted EBITDA declined 44.4% to $24.5 million year over year.

UFP Packaging: Sales declined 1.4% to $370.1 million due to soft industrial activity and volatile lumber pricing. Adjusted EBITDA contracted 26.9% to $27.5 million compared to the year-ago quarter.

UFP Construction: Net sales of $439.8 million, down 9.7% year over year due to soft housing demand and weak consumer sentiment. Adjusted EBITDA tumbled year over year by 26.3% to $33.2 million.

Balance Sheet & Liquidity

Cash and cash equivalents were $914.2 million as of the fourth quarter of 2025, down from $1.17 billion at 2024-end. The current liquidity level is sufficient to meet the short-term obligation of $0.9 million. The long-term debt was $228.9 million as of the fourth quarter 2025-end, slightly down from $229.8 million at 2024-end.

As of Dec. 27, 2025, the company repurchased 4.5 million shares for $443 million (or $98.39 per share).

UFPI’s FY25 Highlights

In 2025, UFPI reported net sales of $6.32 billion, down 5% from the fiscal 2024 level.

The annual gross profit was $1.06 billion, down from $1.23 billion reported in the prior year, while gross margin contracted 160 basis points year over year to 16.8%.

Story Continues

Adjusted EBITDA totaled $563.6 million, down from $682.3 million in fiscal 2024, while the adjusted EBITDA margin contracted 140 basis points year over year to 8.9%.

UFPI’s Long-Term Outlook For 2026

The company expects market conditions to remain challenging in 2026, with demand likely to stay flat to slightly down across segments. Residential construction-related markets are expected to remain weak, while other end markets may stabilize. However, the company believes market share gains, cost-reduction efforts and growth in its Deckorators and Surestone businesses will help it outperform the broader market.

UFP Industries remains committed to its long-term growth strategy. The company still aims to achieve annual unit sales growth of 7-10%, including contributions from bolt-on acquisitions, while driving at least 10% of total sales from new products. Additionally, UFP Industries targets EBITDA margins of 12.5%.

How Have Estimates Been Moving Since Then?

Since the earnings release, investors have witnessed a flat trend in fresh estimates.

VGM Scores

Currently, UFP Industries has a nice Growth Score of B, though it is lagging a lot on the Momentum Score front with a D. However, the stock has a grade of B on the value side, putting it in the top 40% for value investors.

Overall, the stock has an aggregate VGM Score of B. If you aren't focused on one strategy, this score is the one you should be interested in.

Outlook

UFP Industries has a Zacks Rank #5 (Strong Sell). We expect a below average return from the stock in the next few months.

title: UFP Packaging Announces Nail-Gun-Free Crate Line for Safer Assembly
content:
GRAND RAPIDS, Mich., March 24, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- UFP Packaging debuted Slot-Lock and Clamp-Lock 100, two new industrial crate systems engineered for nail-gun-free assembly. The additions expand its pneumatic-free product portfolio alongside U-Loc 200, introduced in 2025.UFP Packaging logo

The new systems improve workplace safety and efficiency by eliminating the need for nail and staple guns, providing safer alternatives to traditional pneumatic construction.

"Even in mature markets like crating, innovation can meaningfully improve how work gets done," said Robert Bilbrough, director of product development. "We are proud to develop solutions that address safety, efficiency and employee satisfaction within our customers' manufacturing environments."

The new crates exceed safety standards without compromising strength or durability. Each system can be disassembled without damaging components, supporting reuse and reducing waste.

Slot-Lock

Slot-Lock is a closed-style crate that uses CNC-routed interlocking panels and secures with strapping. It is suited for aerospace and defense components, medical technology equipment, and sensitive electronics.

Clamp-Lock 100

Clamp-Lock 100 is an open-style crate that uses patent-pending steel L-bracket fasteners designed for hand application during assembly. It is suited for commercial equipment, building materials, and infrastructure components.

U-Loc 200

U-Loc 200 is an open-style crate featuring patented polyamide fasteners designed for hand application and removal. This system is ideal for automotive components as well as heavy machinery and equipment.

UFP Packaging's in-house engineering and design teams provide customizable configurations of each system. For more information, visit https://ufppackaging.com/products/nail-gun-free-crates.

About Robert Bilbrough

Robert Bilbrough is the director of product development at UFP Packaging. Over 12 years at UFP Industries, he has led the development of packaging solutions that address industry pain points and holds multiple patents for his designs. He began his career in 2014 as a specialist after earning his degree in industrial and product design from Auburn University.

About UFP Packaging

UFP Packaging is a leading North American provider of industrial packaging solutions and the largest producer of wooden pallets and crates in the United States. With expertise in metal fabrication, corrugated conversion and labeling, UFP Packaging offers a full range of packaging services, positioning itself as a comprehensive provider. Headquartered in Grand Rapids, Mich., UFP Packaging is a subsidiary of UFP Industries, Inc. (Nasdaq: UFPI).

title: ProWood Backs the Next Generation of Home Developers, Commits to Filling Critical Knowledge and Training Gaps with NHCA Alliance
content:
Lumber industry leader's support of NHCA's first Build the Builder program crucial for addressing housing shortage

GRAND RAPIDS, Mich., March 19, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- ProWood®, a leader in premium pressure-treated lumber, today announced a strategic partnership with the National Hispanic Construction Alliance (NHCA) to expand career pathways for Hispanic builders and trade workers across the United States. A key element of the collaboration includes ProWood's support of NHCA's 2026 launch of Build the Builder, an initiative designed to prepare builders and developers to reinvest in their communities.ProWood / NHCA

"Expanding the pipeline of knowledgeable and capable developers is essential to addressing the nation's urgent housing needs," said Darren Bennett, vice president of sales and marketing at ProWood. "Hispanic construction professionals are a vital part of that future, and NHCA's leadership in supporting their advancement is unmatched. We're proud to partner in strengthening this growing segment of the industry."

NHCA's 2025 State of Hispanics in Construction Report shows that Hispanic builders now make up nearly one-third of the U.S. construction workforce and drove 59% of industry growth between 2018 and 2023. In response to this growing influence, NHCA—founded in 2023 by the National Association of Hispanic Real Estate Professionals (NAHREP) and led by Executive Director Sergio Barajas—has rapidly expanded to provide culturally relevant training, capital pathways, and professional development for Hispanic tradespeople and developers.

"Hispanic workers are driving the future of construction, and they deserve access to the training and partnerships that help them advance," said Barajas. "ProWood's commitment to this community strengthens our ability to deliver meaningful advancement opportunities through programs like Build the Builder."

How ProWood Will Support NHCA's Growing Hispanic Construction Cohort

Through this partnership, ProWood will:

Support the launch of NHCA's Build the Builder program, training Hispanic developers through in-person cohorts focused on real estate fundamentals, financing, project planning, and community reinvestment.  Expand access to industry education by participating in NHCA-led webinars that introduce the program and broaden outreach to potential participants.  Provide executive guidance through ProWood's representation on the NHCA Corporate Advisory Council, where Darren Bennett will help shape training and workforce initiatives that reflect real industry needs.  Support accessible learning through NHCA's translation of program materials for Spanish-speaking members.

Story Continues

The inaugural Build the Builder event will take place May 6–10, 2026, in Los Angeles, California. Learn more or apply.

ABOUT PROWOOD ProWood, a brand of UFP Retail Solutions, LLC, a UFP Industries company, is the industry's foremost manufacturer-distributor of lumber products and premier building materials. With a nationwide presence and a diverse range of products tailored for both building professionals and DIY homeowners, we deliver solutions that meet every need. Backed by industry-leading warranties and a relentless commitment to innovation, ProWood leads the way in education and product expertise, ensuring an exceptional customer experience at every touchpoint.

title: How The UFP Industries (UFPI) Story Is Shifting As Analysts Recut Price Targets
content:
Never miss an important update on your stock portfolio and cut through the noise. Over 7 million investors trust Simply Wall St to stay informed where it matters for FREE.

The latest analyst reset around UFP Industries hinges on a modest price target tweak, with one camp anchoring on US$100 and another trimming to US$110. Bullish voices see the US$100 level as aligned with the company’s fundamentals and medium term potential, while more cautious analysts are tying the US$110 cut to softer Q4 results and lower 2026 expectations. As you read on, you will see how these differing targets shape the evolving story around risk, return, and what might matter most for your watchlist.

Analyst Price Targets don't always capture the full story. Head over to our Company Report to find new ways to value UFP Industries.

What Wall Street Has Been Saying

🐂 Bullish Takeaways

DA Davidson, via analyst Kurt Yinger, maintains a positive stance on UFP Industries even after trimming its price target to US$110 from US$112, signaling that the firm still sees upside potential from recent levels. Stifel’s move to lift its price target to US$100 from US$98 while keeping a Hold rating suggests that, in its view, current execution and valuation are roughly in balance rather than materially stretched.

🐻 Bearish Takeaways

DA Davidson explicitly flagged weaker than expected Q4 results, which it links to a delay in anticipated cyclical demand improvement, highlighting near term execution and demand risk. The same DA Davidson revision shows that longer range forecasts, including 2026 assumptions, have been reset lower, which could limit how much investors are willing to pay for UFP Industries until there is clearer growth visibility.

Do your thoughts align with the Bull or Bear Analysts? Perhaps you think there's more to the story. Head to the Simply Wall St Community to discover more perspectives!NasdaqGS:UFPI 1-Year Stock Price Chart

See how UFP Industries' fair value stacks up across multiple valuation models — not just analyst targets.

What's in the News

UFP Industries completed a share repurchase of 1,904,150 shares, or 3.27% of shares, for a total of US$174.01 million under the buyback announced on July 28, 2025. This included 1,678,934 shares, or 2.88%, acquired between September 28 and December 27, 2025. At the 2026 NAHB International Builders Show, the company highlighted new Deckorators Summit and Altitude decking lines built on its Surestone technology and a fire resistant core with a Class B flame spread rating, along with ProWood TrueFrame Joist for deck substructures. UFP Industries also featured Edge Arris mineral based composite trim using Surestone technology and introduced Frame Forward Systems from UFP Site Built, combining offsite manufactured structural components with onsite installation. The board of directors declared a quarterly cash dividend of US$0.36 per share of common stock, payable on March 16, 2026, to shareholders of record on March 2, 2026.

Story Continues

How This Changes the Fair Value For UFP Industries

Fair Value moves from US$112.20 to US$111.80, a trim of about 0.4% in the model. Revenue Growth stays at 3.61%, effectively unchanged in the updated assumptions. Net Profit Margin shifts from 6.19% to 5.78%, a reduction of around 0.4 percentage points. Future P/E moves from 15.13x to 16.14x, applying a higher multiple to projected earnings. Discount Rate adjusts from 8.45% to 8.43%, indicating only a very small change in the assumed risk profile.

title: 3 Reasons to Sell UFPI and 1 Stock to Buy Instead
content:
Over the past six months, UFP Industries’s shares (currently trading at $90.63) have posted a disappointing 7.7% loss, well below the S&P 500’s 1% gain. This was partly due to its softer quarterly results and might have investors contemplating their next move.

Is now the time to buy UFP Industries, or should you be careful about including it in your portfolio? Check out our in-depth research report to see what our analysts have to say, it’s free.

Why Do We Think UFP Industries Will Underperform?

Even with the cheaper entry price, we're cautious about UFP Industries. Here are three reasons there are better opportunities than UFPI and a stock we'd rather own.

1. Long-Term Revenue Growth Disappoints

A company’s long-term performance is an indicator of its overall quality. Any business can experience short-term success, but top-performing ones enjoy sustained growth for years. Over the last five years, UFP Industries grew its sales at a sluggish 4.2% compounded annual growth rate. This fell short of our benchmark for the industrials sector.UFP Industries Quarterly Revenue

2. EPS Barely Growing

Analyzing the long-term change in earnings per share (EPS) shows whether a company's incremental sales were profitable – for example, revenue could be inflated through excessive spending on advertising and promotions.

UFP Industries’s unimpressive 4.5% annual EPS growth over the last five years aligns with its revenue performance. This tells us it maintained its per-share profitability as it expanded.UFP Industries Trailing 12-Month EPS (Non-GAAP)

3. New Investments Fail to Bear Fruit as ROIC Declines

ROIC, or return on invested capital, is a metric showing how much operating profit a company generates relative to the money it has raised (debt and equity).

We like to invest in businesses with high returns, but the trend in a company’s ROIC is what often surprises the market and moves the stock price. Over the last few years, UFP Industries’s ROIC has unfortunately decreased significantly. We like what management has done in the past, but its declining returns are perhaps a symptom of fewer profitable growth opportunities.UFP Industries Trailing 12-Month Return On Invested Capital

Final Judgment

UFP Industries falls short of our quality standards. After the recent drawdown, the stock trades at 16.9× forward P/E (or $90.63 per share). This valuation multiple is fair, but we don’t have much confidence in the company. There are more exciting stocks to buy at the moment. Let us point you toward a safe-and-steady industrials business benefiting from an upgrade cycle.
